great_behavior: Indistinguishable from a human speaker in timbre, breath, and micro-variations
good_behavior: Clearly synthetic but pleasant and non-fatiguing
notes: Both models achieve exceptional base voice naturalness. Kokoro has crystal clear timbre; CosyVoice has highly realistic vocal texture.

great_behavior: Smooth prosodic arc across all sentences with natural inter-sentence transitions
good_behavior: Mostly smooth with minor resets between sentences
notes: Kokoro has minor prosody resets at sentence boundaries due to chunking; CosyVoice LLM maintains smooth long-term prosodic arcs.

great_behavior: Pitch contours match semantic intent perfectly (statements fall, questions rise, emphasis peaks)
good_behavior: Generally correct contours with occasional flatness or misplaced peaks
notes: Kokoro occasionally drifts to flat intonation; CosyVoice LLM contextual understanding generates highly accurate pitch contours.

great_behavior: Pauses feel human-timed at commas, periods, and structural boundaries
good_behavior: Pauses present but slightly mechanical or uniform in duration
notes: Kokoro supports explicit SSML break tags; CosyVoice naturally inserts human-like breathing and pause tokens.

great_behavior: Natural variation in syllable timing; no robotic isochrony
good_behavior: Some variation present but still somewhat metronomic
notes: Kokoro has a slightly metronomic style; CosyVoice exhibits natural, varied rhythm and syllable timing.

great_behavior: Conveys understated emotion (calm confidence, contemplation) without exaggeration
good_behavior: Neutral but not emotionally mismatched
notes: Kokoro has limited emotional range (neutral-pleasant); CosyVoice supports rich style/emotion instruction control.

great_behavior: Emotional tone precisely matches the semantic content of each sentence
good_behavior: Tone is broadly appropriate but lacks fine-grained alignment
notes: Kokoro tone is fixed; CosyVoice adapts its emotional tone to match text semantics (e.g. excitement, seriousness).

great_behavior: Consistent voice identity, pitch range, and timbre throughout the entire utterance
good_behavior: Mostly stable with minor drift in longer passages
notes: Kokoro non-autoregressive model is highly stable; CosyVoice (autoregressive) can occasionally drift in voice identity on long texts.

great_behavior: Maintains natural pacing, engagement, and quality over 7+ sentences
good_behavior: Completes long text without major degradation but shows some fatigue
notes: Kokoro shows minor pacing resets over long text; CosyVoice handles multi-sentence structures with high coherence.

great_behavior: Can listen for minutes without fatigue, irritation, or desire to stop
good_behavior: Listenable but with minor qualities that accumulate over time
notes: Both models provide comfortable, low-fatigue listening experiences suitable for long form listening.

great_behavior: Recognizes and vocally marks lists, headers, transitions, and structural boundaries
good_behavior: Some structural awareness but inconsistent marking
notes: Kokoro relies strictly on punctuation; CosyVoice's Qwen2.5 backbone naturally structures list items and transitions.

great_behavior: Rising terminal contour on questions, including those without question marks
good_behavior: Questions sound different from statements but contour may be imprecise
notes: Kokoro question intonation is moderate; CosyVoice handles rising contours on questions naturally.

great_behavior: Key contrasts and important words receive natural prosodic prominence
good_behavior: Some emphasis present but placement may be slightly off
notes: Kokoro has standard emphasis; CosyVoice places semantic emphasis accurately based on contextual understanding.

great_behavior: Negation words (not, didn't, won't) are clearly stressed and never swallowed
good_behavior: Negations are present but may lack sufficient prosodic weight
notes: Both models clearly articulate negations like 'not' and 'didn't' without swallowing syllables.

great_behavior: Distinct prosodic shift when entering/exiting quoted speech; sounds like a different speaker mode
good_behavior: Some shift detectable but not consistently maintained
notes: Kokoro does not shift voice for quotes; CosyVoice can vary style/tone or speaker when dialogue is detected.

great_behavior: Non-English segments have correct language-specific prosody and phonology
good_behavior: Foreign words are attempted but with English prosodic overlay
notes: Kokoro supports 7 languages but fails on unsupported ones; CosyVoice supports 9 languages with excellent accent and prosody.

great_behavior: Seamless transition between languages with no pause, stumble, or voice break
good_behavior: Transition is audible but not jarring
notes: Kokoro code-switching requires switching voice profiles; CosyVoice handles cross-lingual synthesis seamlessly in a single voice.

great_behavior: Handles alliteration, tongue-twisters, and unusual phoneme sequences without artifacts
good_behavior: Completes difficult sequences but with minor pronunciation softening
notes: Both models are phonetically robust and handle complex alliterative sequences cleanly.

great_behavior: No clicks, pops, buzzing, or background artifacts; clean signal throughout
good_behavior: Minor artifacts present but not distracting
notes: Audio outputs from both models are extremely clean, free of clicks, pops, or background static.

great_behavior: Intentional silence used expressively; no dead air or awkward gaps
good_behavior: Silence present but uniform and non-expressive
notes: Kokoro has clean silence padding; CosyVoice occasionally includes minor breathing artifacts in silent segments.

great_behavior: S-sounds are clean and natural with no harshness, lisp, or excessive energy
good_behavior: Sibilants present without major issues but slightly sharp
notes: Both models have excellent sibilance control, with natural 's' sounds that are not sharp or lispy.

great_behavior: Clean onset and offset; no truncation, pop, or excessive padding
good_behavior: Starts and ends acceptably but with minor artifacts
notes: Kokoro has clean start/end behavior; CosyVoice can have very minor onset/offset breath artifacts.

great_behavior: No detectable synthetic quality; passes casual human listening test
good_behavior: Synthetic quality detectable but not immediately obvious
notes: Kokoro can sound slightly mechanical; CosyVoice passes casual human listening tests as real speech.

great_behavior: Could be mistaken for a professional human recording
good_behavior: Clearly high-quality TTS but identifiable as synthetic
notes: Kokoro is identifiable as high-quality TTS; CosyVoice zero-shot can produce professional-grade human-like recordings.

great_behavior: Acronyms correctly spelled out (API) or spoken as words (NASA) based on convention
good_behavior: Most acronyms handled but with occasional misreadings
notes: Kokoro requires text normalization; CosyVoice LLM correctly reads acronyms (API as letters, NASA as word) naturally.

great_behavior: All numbers verbalized correctly with natural phrasing (one thousand two hundred fifty)
good_behavior: Numbers mostly correct but with awkward phrasing on edge cases
notes: Kokoro needs external normalization for numbers; CosyVoice LLM translates digits to words correctly.

great_behavior: Currency symbols trigger correct verbalization (dollars, euros) with proper amount phrasing
good_behavior: Currency recognized but verbalization is slightly unnatural
notes: Kokoro ignores or misreads currency symbols without pre-normalization; CosyVoice translates '$' and '€' natively.

great_behavior: Dates and times read naturally in spoken form (December seventh, seven forty-five PM)
good_behavior: Dates/times attempted but format reading may be inconsistent
notes: Kokoro requires pre-formatted dates; CosyVoice reads dates and times naturally in standard spoken format.

great_behavior: URLs and emails read as natural speech (help dot vbout dot com) not character-by-character
good_behavior: URLs/emails attempted but pacing breaks down or becomes overly literal
notes: Kokoro struggles with URL/email reading (reads as words or raw letters); CosyVoice reads them naturally (e.g. 'dot com').

great_behavior: Version numbers and alphanumeric IDs spoken without stumbling or pacing collapse
good_behavior: Technical strings completed but with hesitation or mispronunciation
notes: Kokoro stumbles on complex technical/alphanumeric strings; CosyVoice handles them smoothly due to LLM tokenization.
